refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal and comparative nature of the requirement. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (not a one-time achievement) measured against a specific threshold over a defined period. This removes ambiguity about what 'achieve' means while remaining solution-neutral—it does not prescribe redundancy, failover mechanisms, or specific communication protocols. The FR is atomic: it expresses a single obligation (maintain availability above threshold) without embedded 'and'/'or' conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days of operational use). Calculate availability as (total_uptime / total_period). Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., 0.999). Pass if measured availability ≥ target; fail otherwise. Document any link outages, root causes, and recovery times.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative communication channels before loss of availability occurs.

DP3: Establish hierarchical communication architecture with local autonomous fallback mode that preserves critical teleoperation functions during link unavailability, resuming remote control upon link restoration.
DP4: Implement adaptive bandwidth management and protocol selection that dynamically adjusts communication parameters to maintain minimum viable link availability under varying network conditions.
DP5: Combine redundant pathways (DP1) with quality monitoring (DP2) and local fallback capability (DP3) to achieve target availability through layered resilience.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Implement a layered resilience architecture comprising (1) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, (2) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting, and (3) hierarchical control architecture enabling local autonomous fallback mode during link loss, with seamless resumption of remote teleoperation upon link restoration.

potential_sources: Coupling between failover logic (DP1 component) and link quality monitoring (DP2 component) must be managed via well-defined state machine and handoff protocols to avoid race conditions or oscillation.

Coupling between remote teleoperation commands and local fallback mode (DP3 component) requires clear mode-switching semantics and command buffering to prevent command loss or duplication during transitions.
Coupling between communication pathway selection and adaptive bandwidth management (DP4 component) must be decoupled through abstraction layer to allow independent evolution of each strategy.
Measurement and reporting of link availability metrics must be independent of failover/rerouting logic to ensure unbiased assessment against 0.999 threshold.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is composite but exhibits acceptable coupling because each sub-component (redundancy, monitoring, fallback, adaptation) addresses a distinct failure mode and can be designed with clear interfaces. The primary risk is temporal coupling during mode transitions; this is mitigated by explicit state machine design and comprehensive testing of edge cases.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multiple independent communication pathways are available at the worksite (e.g., cellular, satellite, mesh, wired backup). RISK: If only one pathway exists, DP1 and DP5 cannot be realized; fallback to DP3 (local autonomy only).

ASSUMPTION: Link quality can be measured and predicted with sufficient lead time (seconds to minutes) before actual loss. RISK: If degradation is abrupt or unpredictable, DP2 effectiveness is reduced; failover latency becomes critical.

ASSUMPTION: Local autonomous fallback mode is operationally acceptable and safe under worksite safety plan. RISK: If safety plan prohibits autonomous operation, DP3 cannot be used; availability target may be unachievable.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to capture representative network conditions and failure modes. RISK: Short evaluation periods may not expose rare failure scenarios; measured availability may not reflect true operational reliability.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation system architecture permits hierarchical control (remote + local modes). RISK: If system is designed for remote-only control, DP3 requires significant architectural redesign.

ASSUMPTION: Failover and rerouting latency is acceptable to operator and worksite safety plan (typically <100 ms for safety-critical operations). RISK: If latency requirements are stricter, additional buffering or predictive mechanisms required.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target threshold (0.999) is technically achievable given available infrastructure and failure rates. RISK: If underlying infrastructure has inherent availability <0.999, target is unachievable without additional redundancy investment.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) acceptable failover/rerouting latency during link transitions, (2) whether local autonomous fallback mode is operationally and safety-acceptable, (3) availability of multiple independent communication pathways at the worksite, (4) definition of 'link availability' (e.g., does it include latency/jitter thresholds, or only binary connectivity?), and (5) measurement methodology and observation window for validating the 0.999 threshold. Additionally, clarify whether the 0.999 target applies to the entire teleoperation system or only the communication link itself, as system availability may be lower due to other failure modes (e.g., operator error, equipment malfunction).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all remote command transmission, real-time link quality monitoring, failover logic, and local autonomous fallback coordination during link loss or degradation. Owns the layered resilience architecture including redundant pathways, predictive link monitoring, and seamless resumption protocols.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with physical network layer; Teleoperation monitors link quality metrics, triggers failover between redundant pathways, and receives link status updates for proactive rerouting decisions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives remote operator commands and teleoperation directives; Teleoperation translates these into vehicle control signals, buffering and queuing during link degradation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and link quality metrics back to remote operator; includes predictive degradation indicators and fallback mode status.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services; Teleoperation enforces safety bounds in local autonomous fallback mode and reports safety-critical link failures.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Teleoperation is the natural owner of remote command pathways, link quality monitoring, failover logic, and seamless resumption. The layered resilience architecture (redundant pathways, predictive monitoring, local fallback) is core to teleoperation's mission. Minor diffusion: Vehicle must implement local autonomous fallback execution (owned by Vehicle), and Safety Services must provide safety constraints (owned by Safety Services), but orchestration and failover decision logic remain in Teleoperation.

Poor fit as primary owner. Vehicle owns local autonomous fallback execution and hierarchical control during link loss, but does not own remote command reception, link quality monitoring, or failover pathway selection. Placing the entire DP here would scatter teleoperation resilience logic across Vehicle, creating tight coupling between vehicle control and network management. Vehicle should be a consumer of Teleoperation's failover decisions, not the owner.

Poor fit. Safety Services owns safety constraint enforcement and alert generation, but does not own network resilience, link monitoring, or failover logic. Placing DP5 here would conflate safety policy with network architecture, violating separation of concerns. Safety Services should provide safety bounds that Teleoperation and Vehicle respect during fallback, not own the resilience mechanism itself.

Poor fit as primary owner. NGHE Solution is a system-level container, not a functional module. Placing DP5 here would avoid assigning clear ownership and would diffuse resilience logic across multiple sub-components without a coherent boundary. Teleoperation is the appropriate functional module within NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation is the most specific and cohesive boundary for DP5. It owns the end-to-end resilience architecture: (1) redundant communication pathways and automatic failover are intrinsic to remote command delivery; (2) real-time link quality monitoring and predictive degradation detection are prerequisites for intelligent failover decisions; (3) hierarchical control enabling local autonomous fallback is coordinated by Teleoperation, which signals Vehicle to enter fallback mode and Safety Services to enforce constraints. The DP is contained within Teleoperation's functional scope with minimal diffusion to Vehicle (fallback execution) and Safety Services (constraint provision).
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation may become a large, complex module if all failover logic, redundancy management, and link monitoring are implemented monolithically. Mitigation: Decompose Teleoperation internally into sub-modules (e.g., LinkMonitor, FailoverController, LocalFallbackCoordinator) while maintaining a single IM boundary.

Risk: Vehicle must implement local autonomous fallback execution, creating a dependency on Teleoperation's failover signals. If Vehicle's fallback logic is not well-decoupled, changes to Teleoperation's failover strategy could ripple into Vehicle. Mitigation: Define a clear, stable interface (e.g., FallbackModeCommand) between Teleoperation and Vehicle.

Risk: Safety Services must provide real-time safety constraints to Teleoperation during link loss. If Safety Services is slow or unavailable, Teleoperation's fallback decisions may be delayed. Mitigation: Pre-cache safety constraints in Teleoperation and use conservative defaults during Safety Services unavailability.

Risk: Network Link layer must support multiple redundant pathways and expose link quality metrics. If the underlying network does not provide these capabilities, Teleoperation's resilience architecture cannot be fully realized. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface contract includes redundancy and quality-of-service (QoS) reporting.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (link availability ≥ 0.999 over agreed evaluation period). The IM boundary (Teleoperation) owns the mechanisms to achieve this: redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, failover logic, and seamless resumption. Verification can be conducted by instrumenting Teleoperation's link quality telemetry and measuring uptime across the evaluation window. Safety plan compliance is external to this IM but must be coordinated via SafetyAlert interface.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Teleoperation is the unambiguous owner of the layered resilience architecture. The FR-to-IM mapping is direct: maintaining link availability is Teleoperation's primary functional responsibility. External dependencies (Vehicle fallback execution, Safety Services constraint provision, Network Link redundancy) are well-defined via interfaces and do not obscure Teleoperation's ownership.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 is a coherent, complete solution to the FR. It addresses all three pillars of resilience: (1) redundant pathways with automatic failover directly maintain availability during single-point failures; (2) real-time monitoring and predictive degradation enable proactive rerouting before availability drops below threshold; (3) hierarchical control with local autonomous fallback ensures continuity during transient link loss. The DP is expressed at the appropriate level of abstraction—it specifies the logical architecture without prescribing implementation details (e.g., specific protocols, hardware). Traceability to acceptance criterion is strong: the combination of redundancy, monitoring, and fallback directly supports achieving ≥0.999 availability.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Teleoperation's internal complexity. The DP bundles three distinct concerns (redundancy management, link monitoring, fallback coordination). If implemented monolithically, Teleoperation becomes a large, difficult-to-test module. Mitigation: Decompose internally into cohesive sub-modules (LinkMonitor, FailoverController, LocalFallbackCoordinator) while maintaining a single IM boundary and stable external interfaces.

Moderate risk: Vehicle-Teleoperation coupling. Vehicle must execute local autonomous fallback upon Teleoperation's signal. If Vehicle's fallback logic is tightly coupled to Teleoperation's failover strategy, changes to failover thresholds or rerouting logic could require Vehicle modifications. Mitigation: Define a stable, minimal FallbackModeCommand interface (e.g., {mode: FALLBACK|RESUME, constraints: SafetyBounds, timestamp}) that decouples strategy from execution.

Moderate risk: Safety Services latency. Teleoperation depends on real-time safety constraints from Safety Services to enforce bounds during fallback. If Safety Services is unavailable or slow, Teleoperation's fallback decisions may be delayed, impacting availability. Mitigation: Pre-cache safety constraints in Teleoperation at startup and during normal operation; use conservative defaults (e.g., reduced speed, geofence) during Safety Services unavailability.

Low risk: Network Link capability assumption. DP5 assumes the Network Link layer provides redundant pathways and exposes link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength). If the underlying network does not support these, Teleoperation cannot fully realize the DP. Mitigation: Validate Network Link interface contract early; ensure it includes redundancy capability and QoS reporting. If not available, escalate as a design constraint violation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural, cohesive owner of the entire DP. Remote command pathways, link quality monitoring, failover logic, and seamless resumption are all intrinsic to teleoperation's functional scope. Minor diffusion to Vehicle (fallback execution) and Safety Services (constraint provision) is unavoidable and well-managed via clear interfaces. No evidence of scattered responsibilities or redundant implementations across modules. The IM boundary is appropriately scoped: it does not over-claim ownership of Vehicle control or Safety policy, nor does it under-claim ownership of network resilience orchestration.

recommended_revisions: Decompose Teleoperation internally into three sub-modules: (1) LinkMonitor—owns link quality metrics collection, degradation prediction, and threshold evaluation; (2) FailoverController—owns redundant pathway selection, rerouting decisions, and failover orchestration; (3) LocalFallbackCoordinator—owns fallback mode signaling to Vehicle and constraint enforcement. Maintain a single external IM boundary with stable interfaces to Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Vehicle.

Formalize the FallbackModeCommand interface between Teleoperation and Vehicle. Specify: (a) trigger conditions (link loss duration, quality threshold breach); (b) command payload (mode: FALLBACK|RESUME, safety constraints, timestamp, expected resumption time); (c) acknowledgment protocol (Vehicle confirms fallback entry/exit). Document that Vehicle owns fallback execution logic, not Teleoperation.

Establish a SafetyConstraintCache in Teleoperation. Define: (a) cache refresh interval (e.g., every 100 ms during normal operation); (b) fallback defaults if Safety Services is unavailable (e.g., max speed 0.5 m/s, geofence radius 10 m); (c) timeout threshold for Safety Services responses (e.g., 500 ms). Document that Safety Services owns constraint generation, Teleoperation owns caching and fallback application.

Validate Network Link interface contract. Ensure it specifies: (a) redundant pathway availability (e.g., primary + backup links); (b) link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength, jitter); (c) failover trigger mechanism (automatic or Teleoperation-initiated); (d) QoS guarantees during failover (e.g., latency < 200 ms, loss < 1%). If not available, escalate as a design constraint violation and revise DP5 or FR accordingly.

Add acceptance criterion detail: Define 'agreed evaluation period' (e.g., 30 days of continuous operation) and 'customer-defined target threshold' (e.g., 0.999 = 43.2 seconds downtime per month). Specify measurement methodology: (a) link availability = (total_time - downtime) / total_time; (b) downtime = periods where link is unavailable OR link quality falls below minimum threshold for remote operation; (c) measurement granularity (e.g., 1-second samples). Document in test plan.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR5, DP5, and Teleoperation IM are sound and well-integrated (all gates pass, containment score 0.92). However, the recommended_revisions identify three critical internal decompositions within Teleoperation that must be derived as explicit sub-FRs before implementation begins. These are not optional refinements—they are mandatory architectural decisions that affect safety criticality, testability, and interface stability. The high safety criticality of this system (teleoperation failure can lead to loss of vehicle control) demands that the three sub-modules (LinkMonitor, FailoverController, LocalFallbackCoordinator) be explicitly designed, verified, and owned. Deriving sub-FRs for each ensures traceability, clear ownership, and independent verification of each resilience pillar. Additionally, the SafetyConstraintCache and FallbackModeCommand interface formalization require explicit functional requirements to drive their design. Stopping here would leave Teleoperation as a monolithic black box; decomposing ensures architectural clarity and safety assurance.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Monitor link quality in real-time and predict degradation to enable proactive failover decisions before availability threshold is breached. Acceptance: LinkMonitor reports latency, packet loss, signal strength, and jitter at ≥10 Hz; degradation prediction accuracy ≥90% within 5-second lookahead window.

FR5.2: Select and activate redundant command pathways automatically upon link quality degradation or loss, ensuring seamless transition without command loss or duplication. Acceptance: FailoverController switches to backup pathway within 100 ms of trigger; no command loss or duplication during transition; failover success rate ≥99.9% across 1000+ test cycles.

FR5.3: Signal Vehicle to enter autonomous fallback mode with safety-constrained execution when teleoperation link is unavailable, and coordinate resumption when link is restored. Acceptance: LocalFallbackCoordinator sends FallbackModeCommand within 50 ms of failover trigger; Vehicle acknowledges fallback entry within 200 ms; resumption is seamless with no safety constraint violations.

FR5.4: Cache safety constraints locally in Teleoperation and apply conservative defaults during Safety Services unavailability to ensure fallback decisions are never blocked by external service latency. Acceptance: SafetyConstraintCache refreshes every 100 ms during normal operation; fallback defaults (max speed 0.5 m/s, geofence 10 m) are applied within 50 ms if Safety Services response exceeds 500 ms timeout; cache hit rate ≥99.5%.

FR5.5: Formalize and enforce the FallbackModeCommand interface between Teleoperation and Vehicle to decouple failover strategy from fallback execution and enable independent evolution. Acceptance: Interface specification includes trigger conditions, command payload schema, acknowledgment protocol; Vehicle implementation passes contract compliance tests; interface remains stable across ≥3 major Teleoperation revisions.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but must be refined to explicitly address the three sub-module decomposition (LinkMonitor, FailoverController, LocalFallbackCoordinator) and the two critical interfaces (FallbackModeCommand, SafetyConstraintCache). Revise DP5 to state: 'Teleoperation implements layered resilience via three cohesive sub-modules: (1) LinkMonitor continuously observes link quality metrics and predicts degradation; (2) FailoverController orchestrates automatic pathway switching and rerouting based on LinkMonitor signals; (3) LocalFallbackCoordinator signals Vehicle fallback mode via stable FallbackModeCommand interface and enforces safety constraints via pre-cached SafetyConstraintCache. This decomposition maintains a single external IM boundary while enabling independent verification and evolution of each resilience pillar.' This revision ensures DP5 is implementable and traceable to the sub-FRs.

assumptions: Network Link layer provides redundant pathways (primary + backup) and exposes link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength, jitter) via a stable interface.
Vehicle is capable of executing autonomous fallback mode upon receiving FallbackModeCommand and can acknowledge fallback entry/exit within 200 ms.

Safety Services can provide real-time safety constraints (speed limits, geofence bounds) at ≥10 Hz during normal operation; Teleoperation can tolerate up to 500 ms latency before applying conservative defaults.

Teleoperation has sufficient computational capacity to run LinkMonitor (≥10 Hz sampling), FailoverController (≥100 Hz decision loop), and LocalFallbackCoordinator (≥50 Hz signaling) concurrently without exceeding latency budgets.

The 'agreed evaluation period' for link availability measurement is defined by customer (e.g., 30 days continuous operation) and measurement methodology is documented in test plan before verification begins.
open_risks: Safety criticality risk: If SafetyConstraintCache defaults are too conservative (e.g., max speed 0.5 m/s), Vehicle may be unable to execute critical fallback maneuvers (e.g., emergency stop, obstacle avoidance). Mitigation: Validate fallback defaults against Vehicle's minimum safe operating envelope; involve Safety Engineering in default selection.

Coupling risk: Vehicle's fallback logic may be tightly coupled to Teleoperation's failover thresholds or rerouting strategy. If Teleoperation changes failover trigger conditions, Vehicle may not behave as expected. Mitigation: Formalize FallbackModeCommand interface early; conduct integration testing with Vehicle across multiple failover scenarios.

Latency risk: LinkMonitor's degradation prediction (5-second lookahead) may be insufficient if network conditions degrade faster than predicted. Failover may occur too late, causing brief link loss before backup pathway is active. Mitigation: Validate prediction accuracy empirically; consider shorter lookahead window (2-3 seconds) if prediction confidence is low.

Safety Services dependency risk: If Safety Services is unavailable during normal operation, Teleoperation must rely on pre-cached constraints. If cache is stale or incorrect, fallback decisions may violate safety policy. Mitigation: Implement cache freshness monitoring; escalate to operator if cache age exceeds threshold (e.g., >5 minutes); define fallback defaults conservatively.

Network Link interface risk: If Network Link does not support redundant pathways or does not expose link quality metrics, DP5 cannot be realized. Mitigation: Validate Network Link interface contract early in design; if not available, escalate as design constraint violation and revise FR5 or DP5.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification: Confirm redundant pathway availability, link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength, jitter), failover trigger mechanism, and QoS guarantees.
Vehicle fallback capability assessment: Confirm Vehicle can execute autonomous fallback mode, acknowledge FallbackModeCommand within 200 ms, and operate safely under conservative safety constraints.
Safety Services interface specification: Confirm constraint provision latency (target <100 ms, max 500 ms), constraint schema, and availability SLA during normal operation.
LinkMonitor degradation prediction validation: Empirical testing of prediction accuracy across multiple network degradation scenarios; confirm 5-second lookahead is achievable with ≥90% accuracy.

Failover latency measurement: Instrumentation of FailoverController to measure pathway switch time, command transmission latency, and Vehicle acknowledgment latency; confirm all are within budgets (100 ms, 50 ms, 200 ms respectively).
SafetyConstraintCache performance testing: Confirm cache refresh rate (≥10 Hz), fallback default application latency (<50 ms), and cache hit rate (≥99.5%).
FallbackModeCommand interface contract: Formal specification of trigger conditions, command payload schema, acknowledgment protocol; Vehicle implementation compliance testing.

Integration testing: End-to-end failover scenarios with Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link; measure link availability over agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days); confirm ≥0.999 availability target is met.
